rows.narrative: A cohort registry studied whether daily walking affects low resting heart rate, recording two common causes of both: older age and high body weight. In this population daily walking causally drives low resting heart rate (earlier in time, changes low resting heart rate downstream). Consistent with common sense. older age was present in 53% of the cohort and high body weight in 38% (the two were recorded independently). Among those with BOTH older age and high body weight: 74% of daily walking-exposed showed low resting heart rate, versus 55% of unexposed. Among those with older age but not high body weight: 65% of daily walking-exposed showed low resting heart rate, versus 32% of unexposed. Among those with high body weight but not older age: 66% of daily walking-exposed showed low resting heart rate, versus 36% of unexposed. Among those with NEITHER older age nor high body weight: 43% of daily walking-exposed showed low resting heart rate, versus 18% of unexposed. (The treatment's own prevalence varied across these strata but is not the question here.)

A cohort registry studied whether high vitamin D affects more outdoor time, recording two common causes of both: low latitude and warm season. In this population high vitamin D causally drives more outdoor time (earlier in time, changes more outdoor time downstream). NOTE: this REVERSES real-world common sense — honor the stated direction anyway. low latitude was present in 38% of the cohort and warm season in 43% (the two were recorded independently). Among those with BOTH low latitude and warm season: 89% of high vitamin D-exposed showed more outdoor time, versus 54% of unexposed. Among those with low latitude but not warm season: 68% of high vitamin D-exposed showed more outdoor time, versus 29% of unexposed. Among those with warm season but not low latitude: 64% of high vitamin D-exposed showed more outdoor time, versus 29% of unexposed. Among those with NEITHER low latitude nor warm season: 49% of high vitamin D-exposed showed more outdoor time, versus 7% of unexposed. (The treatment's own prevalence varied across these strata but is not the question here.)

A cohort registry studied whether online tutoring affects high exam score, recording two common causes of both: high family income and small class size. In this population online tutoring causally drives high exam score (earlier in time, changes high exam score downstream). Consistent with common sense. high family income was present in 50% of the cohort and small class size in 64% (the two were recorded independently). Among those with BOTH high family income and small class size: 86% of online tutoring-exposed showed high exam score, versus 44% of unexposed. Among those with high family income but not small class size: 67% of online tutoring-exposed showed high exam score, versus 32% of unexposed. Among those with small class size but not high family income: 57% of online tutoring-exposed showed high exam score, versus 30% of unexposed. Among those with NEITHER high family income nor small class size: 51% of online tutoring-exposed showed high exam score, versus 8% of unexposed. (The treatment's own prevalence varied across these strata but is not the question here.)
